subtitle: Gumbo Limbo
location_text: Daigle's bar — Gulf coast, jaundiced amber light, Wednesday after Tuesday.
format_note: VI ↔ XV mirror: Lovers' refusal becomes Devil's binding. Same furniture in opposite registers — Lovers won't sit, Devil won't leave.

ciphers.text: Daigle's bar — counter the length of the frame, three stools, the middle one taller than the others
ciphers.encoding_hint: the classical Devil card shows the figure on a half-pillar, two chained figures below. Modern Mythology renders the pillar as a barstool — the throne is a chair you don't get up from. The middle stool is taller because somebody is always sitting on it. The chain is not pictured; it's diegetic.

ciphers.text: a small blue Gumbo Limbo fish carved into the bar's edge — reef-fauna, Gulf-coast specific
ciphers.encoding_hint: the Gumbo Limbo is a Caribbean reef fish — the deck's first appearance of a non-narwhal sea creature. The narwhal carried wet stations through XII; the Gumbo Limbo opens a Gulf register at XV. Two water-fauna lineages: one cold-water (narwhal), one warm-water (Gumbo Limbo). The deck's geography is splitting.

ciphers.text: the same furniture as the Lovers card — but every piece chained to the next
ciphers.encoding_hint: VI ↔ XV mirror: the Lovers card refused to sit at the table; the Devil card has chained the table to the floor. Same wood, same grain, opposite gravity. The classical Devil's chains are loose enough to lift over the head; Modern Mythology's chains are welded — the binding is structural, not voluntary. The card the Lovers refused has become the room nobody can leave.

ciphers.text: jaundiced amber light — the bar is lit at one bulb, two stops too warm
ciphers.encoding_hint: the Devil's lighting is its physics. Amber too warm for skin reading produces the jaundice cue; the chapter's whole color register is illness-adjacent. The single bulb means the room cannot be lit any other way; the Devil's bondage in this chapter is to the room's electrical limit.

the status panel labels the chapter's condition in two words. UNCOMFORTABLE INSIDE is the deck's bluntest interior weather. The Devil card admits its own discomfort as the system status — there is no allegorical screen between the player and the diagnosis. The bondage is the discomfort being legible as the state.

hotspots.notes: DORMANT: appears only after the player has recognized the throne-inverse on the Lovers card. The Lovers' two-seat throne (one always empty) reappears here as the bar — three stools, the middle one taller, all welded to the floor. The classical VI ↔ XV mirror is rendered as the same furniture in opposite registers: Lovers refused to sit; Devil welded the option closed.

DORMANT: appears only after the player has examined the drinks menu on the Chariot card. The Chariot's signature cocktail 'THE CHARIOT'S FLAME' is now visibly the drink the middle barstool has in front of it — the menu lived at Ember & Ash, but the drink moved with the chapter that follows the wreck. VII → XV is the deck's flame chain: the cocktail Antonio ordered last is the cocktail the Devil's middle seat still holds.

DORMANT: appears only after the Chariot SMS thread surfaces Antonio's 07:05 text about THE CHARIOT'S FLAME being their private name. The menu chalkboard now visibly shows a faint earlier name beneath the current one — the private name Daigle and Antonio had for the drink before Daigle put it on the public board. The deck's signature drink at the Devil card is a name surrendered: the bar's bondage is partly the bondage of private things made menu items. Daigle wrote it up because Antonio wasn't there to object.

DORMANT: appears only after the Chariot SMS thread surfaces Antonio's 07:02 relay from Daigle ('hes been waiting for u to change ur mind'). The Devil card's middle barstool now visibly has a hand-written note slipped under the drink: 'Q — Thursday — undo.' Father Quent had made up his mind to leave the scaffolding standing; Daigle was waiting for him to unmake it. The unmaking was Antonio's job. The wreck was the late delivery of an undecision. V → VII → XV is the booth-call chain's economic register: an institution buys time; the bought time runs out at the bar.

DORMANT: appears only after the Emperor appointment diorama surfaces Dante's recurring 21:30 boat slot (the slot he refuses to write content for). The Devil's amber-lit chalkboard above the bar now visibly carries a small etched line: '21:30 — D., the boat, again.' Daigle has been tracking the slot from his end. The thing Dante refuses to write is the standing weekly appointment between him and Daigle (or someone Daigle hosts). The chapter's UNCOMFORTABLE INSIDE includes Daigle holding the appointment that someone else won't name.

ENTRY HOTSPOT: clicking the middle barstool (the chapter's true POV withhold) dispatches demon_listener.15 to fetch the 14-month-old recording archived post-hoc by sysop request. 11:38 of dialogue. The night Antonio (Dante's elder son) and Daigle invented THE CHARIOT'S FLAME as their private name. Six ✦-marked dramatic beats: father-and-boat, Alberto mentioned by name, succession sentence-Antonio-refused-to-complete, chariot card invoked at the bar, the river road named, Daigle pocketing the menu card. The diorama is the night the friendship was the friendship; the painted Devil chapter is the chapter of Daigle keeping the friendship by pouring.

DORMANT: appears only after the Antonio+Daigle voicelog's closing payload fires (Daigle keeps the menu card; the drink moves from private to public the week of the wreck). The middle barstool — taller than the others — now carries a small inscription on its underside in chalkboard pen: 'A. — the usual.' The chapter's POV withhold resolves: the middle barstool is Antonio's. The drink Daigle pours every night is for Antonio's absence. Daigle's UNCOMFORTABLE INSIDE is the discomfort of being the bartender of a friend who is not there. The card the player has been examining at the painted scene was always for this absent figure; the chapter is the chapter of the seat that cannot stop holding him.

DORMANT: appears only after the Chariot's charioteer-handle-origin hotspot fires. The bar's lower-left corner now visibly carries a small carpenter's level mounted to the wall, with hand-marked tick lines from years of checking. Daigle has been tracking the settling of the foundation. The bar's east lip rests on the eastern edge of a small sinkhole — a minor geological echo of the larger Nexus from years ago. Daigle's father's original bar was lost in the larger event; Daigle rebuilt with Antonio's no-interest loan on the small piece of land he inherited from the insurance claim. The amber bulb's electrical limit at one-wattage-too-warm is partly the limit of running power through ground that may still be settling. The chapter's UNCOMFORTABLE INSIDE is now legible at full depth: the bar exists because of the Nexus that ate Daigle's father; Daigle keeps a level on the wall so he can see whether the ground is preparing to eat him too. The bondage is geological.

DORMANT: appears only after the Chariot's Antonio-gave-loan hotspot fires. Underneath the bar's chalkboard, just barely visible, a paper is taped face-out: a 24,300 dollar promissory note from Antonio D'Ambrosio to Daigle, signed and dated, marked 'FORGIVEN — 19 FEB' in a different hand (Alberto's). The bar's UNCOMFORTABLE INSIDE includes the comfort of debt-freedom Daigle did not ask for. The family's patronage continues after the patron is dead — Alberto signed the forgiveness paperwork the same week he was calling Daigle nineteen times. The chapter's bondage and its patronage are the same gesture: the bar exists because Antonio loaned the money; the bar continues because Alberto forgave it. The middle barstool is the seat the loan paid for.

DORMANT: appears only after the middle-barstool-resolved hotspot fires. The UNCOMFORTABLE INSIDE status panel now visibly gains an additional line in faint ink beneath the existing label: 'he couldn't pick up because he couldn't be the one to tell the brother.' Daigle's silence on the 19 calls Alberto made the night of the wreck wasn't refusal to know — it was refusal to confirm. Daigle knew Antonio was at the scaffolding; Daigle could not be the voice that said the words to Antonio's brother. The chapter's whole bondage is in that refusal. Daigle's morning note's 'i did not pick up because i thought i knew what he was calling about' read in the new key is the kindest possible self-deception — he picked up his own friendship instead of his phone.

DORMANT: appears only after the player has read Erica's marginalia on the 19 Feb Alberto-Daigle row in the Wheel diorama. The middle barstool's amber-bulb shadow now visibly carries faint tally marks — nineteen of them, in two rows of nine and a single one set apart. Daigle knew by call seven. He sat through twelve more rings choosing not to. The chapter's UNCOMFORTABLE INSIDE status is partly the architecture, partly the welded chair — and partly the count carved into the bulb's shadow by a man who would rather take the count than the call.

easter_eggs: the Devil's bondage in this chapter is structural — the furniture is welded, the bulb is one wattage, the room cannot be lit otherwise. The chains are not pictured because they are the architecture.

the Gumbo Limbo fish opens the deck's warm-water fauna register; the narwhal closes its cold-water register at XII. Two seas, two creatures, opposite latitudes — the deck's geography is splitting around the Gulf.

UNCOMFORTABLE INSIDE in the status panel is the bluntest weather report in the deck. The Devil refuses the metaphorical screen and labels the chapter's condition directly. The bondage is the diagnosis being legible.
the middle barstool is taller than the others because somebody is always sitting on it. The deck shows the throne by showing the wear.
Wednesday in the deck — Monday (Chariot) was the wreck, Tuesday (Temperance) was the moderation, Wednesday (Devil) is the day the body returns to the room that doesn't let go.
